title: Design a Cloud Storage Service Like Dropbox

transcript.text: Design a file sync and storage service: upload/download files, sync across a user's devices, share files, version history. Focus on file chunking and dedup, sync, the metadata vs blob split, and conflict resolution.

blocks.md:
# Design a Cloud Storage Service Like Dropbox

> The problem: a file sync + storage service. Users upload and download files, those files sync automatically across all of a user's devices, they can share files with others, and they can roll back to old versions. The interesting engineering lives in four places — chunking files and de-duplicating them so we only ship what changed, splitting metadata from the raw bytes, detecting and propagating changes fast, and resolving conflicts when two devices edit the same file offline.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale first, then sketch the API and data model, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — upload, sync, share, versioning. After that I'll go deep on the two or three hardest parts: chunking and dedup, the sync engine, and conflict resolution — checking in with you as I go. I'll target an Amazon-style bar, so cost and operations are first-class and I'll work backwards from what the customer actually feels. Sound good?"

The core tension of Dropbox: a file lives in many places at once — on a phone, a laptop, a desktop, and in the cloud — and they must all *converge* to the same bytes quickly and cheaply, even though devices go offline, edit the same file, and re-upload gigabytes of data we may already have.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Upload a file (chunked, resumable, deduplicated).** A user adds or edits a file and it lands durably in the cloud, uploading only the parts that actually changed.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I save a 2 GB video, lose Wi-Fi at 90%, reconnect, and it picks up where it left off — and if I just renamed it, basically nothing re-uploads."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we must split files into chunks, recognize chunks we already have (content-addressing), and survive a dropped connection mid-upload without restarting from byte zero.

**FR2 — Download / sync a file to a device.** When a file changes anywhere, every other device the user owns pulls the new version automatically.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I edit a doc on my laptop and ten seconds later it's updated on my phone without me touching anything."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** 300 million devices each need to learn "something changed for you" fast, without us hammering the metadata DB with constant polling.

**FR3 — Detect & propagate changes (the sync engine).** The system computes what changed for a given device and tells it exactly which chunks to fetch, using a per-user change journal and a cursor.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Each device remembers a bookmark of how far it's caught up; we hand it the list of things that happened after that bookmark."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** changes must be ordered per user, idempotent on retry, and a device that's been offline a week must catch up without replaying a million events.

**FR4 — Share a file or folder with other users.** A user grants another user (or a public link) read or write access to a file or folder.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I share a folder with my teammate; it now shows up in their Dropbox and syncs to their devices too."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** a shared folder is now in *two* users' change journals; an edit by one must fan out to everyone with access, and permission checks must be cheap on every read.

**FR5 — Version history & conflict resolution.** The system keeps prior versions and resolves the case where two devices edit the same file while offline.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If my laptop and my phone both edited the budget while offline, I don't silently lose one — I get both, clearly labeled."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** there's no single source of truth at edit time; we need a deterministic, never-lose-data conflict policy and cheap storage of old versions.

#### Below the line (out of scope for this session)
- Real-time collaborative editing inside a doc (Google-Docs-style OT/CRDT cursors) — we do file-level sync, not character-level merge.
- Full-text search inside file contents, OCR, thumbnails/transcoding.
- Billing, quotas enforcement UI, admin consoles, team/enterprise SSO.
- Client-side end-to-end encryption (we'll note where it would change dedup).
- Mobile camera-roll auto-backup specifics, LAN sync.

### Non-Functional (talkable targets)

**NFR1 — Durability of stored bytes: 11 nines (99.999999999%).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you store ten million files with us, statistically you'd expect to lose less than one file every ten thousand years."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** we lose a customer's only copy of a file — the single most trust-destroying thing a storage service can do.

**NFR2 — Sync latency: p99 change visible on other devices in under ~10 seconds.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 times out of 100, an edit shows up on your other devices in under ten seconds."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** sync feels broken; users start emailing files to themselves — the whole product value evaporates.

**NFR3 — Availability of the metadata/control plane: four nines (99.99%).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The 'what files do I have' service is down less than about 52 minutes per year total."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** even if bytes are safe, users can't list, upload, or sync — the app looks dead.

**NFR4 — Read-your-own-writes consistency for a single device's own file list.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Right after I save a file, *my* device must see it in the list — no 'where did it go?' moment."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** users think uploads failed and re-upload, doubling load and panicking.

**NFR5 — Resumable, bandwidth-efficient transfer; never re-upload unchanged bytes.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Changing one line in a 1 GB file uploads a few kilobytes, not a gigabyte."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** mobile users blow through data caps, uploads take forever, and our egress/ingress bill explodes.

> 💬 "The whole design is a fight between two enemies: re-shipping bytes we already have, and telling 300 million devices what changed without melting the database. Chunking-plus-dedup kills the first; a change journal plus push notifications kills the second."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "At its core this is a file sync service, so my functional requirements are upload, download-and-sync, the change-detection engine that drives sync, sharing, and version history with conflict resolution. The non-functionals that actually shape the design are durability — this is someone's only copy, so eleven nines — and sync latency, which I'll hold to about ten seconds at p99. I'm explicitly leaving out in-document collaborative editing and search, because file-level sync is already the meaty part. The thing I'll keep coming back to is bandwidth: we must never re-upload bytes we already have, and we must never poll the database to death — those two constraints drive almost every later decision."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Are files mostly large media like photos and videos, or small documents — and what's the average account size?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "The file size distribution decides my chunk size and whether dedup even pays off — big media chunks differently than thousands of tiny docs."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If LARGE media → bigger chunks (4 MB), dedup helps less within a file but cross-user dedup on shared media still helps; egress dominates cost.
  - If SMALL docs → many tiny metadata rows, metadata DB is the bottleneck, dedup within a file (delta sync) pays huge.
- **Assumption:** mixed, skewed toward documents and media; average account ~15 GB; I'll use 4 MB content-defined chunks.

**Q2 — "How fast must a change appear on other devices — is sub-second required, or is 'within a few seconds' fine?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Sub-second forces a persistent push channel to every device; a few seconds lets me use cheaper long-poll."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If sub-second / near-real-time → WebSockets to every device, heavier connection fleet.
  - If a few seconds OK → long-poll with a short hold, far cheaper at 300M devices.
- **Assumption:** ~10 seconds p99 is fine; I'll use long-poll with WebSocket as an upgrade for foreground apps.

**Q3 — "Do we need to support files edited offline on two devices at once, and what's the conflict policy?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Offline-multi-edit is the single hardest correctness case; if it's out of scope the design shrinks a lot."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If YES → I need deterministic conflict detection (version vectors) and a never-lose-data "conflicted copy" policy.
  - If NO (last-writer-wins acceptable) → much simpler, just a version number per file.
- **Assumption:** YES — offline editing is core to Dropbox; I'll create conflicted copies, never silently overwrite.

**Q4 — "Is sharing one-to-one and small-team, or could a single folder be shared with millions (viral public link)?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "A folder shared with millions is a fan-out hot-spot — like a celebrity follower problem — and changes my notification design."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If small teams → fan-out the change into each member's journal directly.
  - If massive public share → can't fan-out to millions; switch to fan-out-on-read for huge shares.
- **Assumption:** mostly small teams (under a few hundred), but I'll design the hot-share escape hatch.

**Q5 — "Strong consistency for metadata, or is brief staleness across regions acceptable?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Strong cross-region consistency is expensive; for a file list, eventual-with-read-your-own-writes is usually enough."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If strong global → single write region, higher latency for far users.
  - If eventual OK → multi-region reads, route writes to user's home region.
- **Assumption:** eventual across regions, but strong read-your-own-writes within a user's home shard.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume mixed file sizes, ~10-second sync, real offline-conflict handling, mostly small-team sharing, and eventual cross-region consistency with read-your-own-writes per user. If any of those is wrong — especially the conflict policy — tell me now, because it changes the data model. Otherwise I'll size it."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I computed these by writing and running a `capacity.py`; here are the rounded results. (QPS = queries per second, how many requests hit the server each second. DAU = daily active users.)

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DAU | 500M users x 20% open a synced device daily | ~100M | "a hundred million active users" | overall fleet sizing |
| Connected devices | 100M DAU x 3 devices/user | ~300M | "three hundred million live connections" | the notification fleet — biggest control-plane cost |
| Change-event QPS (peak) | 100M DAU x 50 changes/day ÷ 86,400s = ~58K/s; x5 peak | ~290K/s | "about three hundred thousand changes a second at peak" | metadata write throughput → sharding |
| Chunk uploads/s (peak) | 290K changes/s x 1 chunk x (1 − 40% dedup) | ~174K/s | "about a hundred seventy thousand blob writes a second" | object-store ingest sizing; dedup saves 40% |
| Read/sync QPS (peak) | 100M x 20 pulls/day ÷ 86,400 = ~23K/s; x5 | ~116K/s | "about a hundred thousand sync pulls a second" | read replicas + CDN |
| Notify servers | 300M conns ÷ 100K conns/server | ~3,000 | "around three thousand notification boxes" | the long-poll/WS fleet |
| **Metadata write QPS (peak)** | **290K changes/s x 2 rows (file + journal)** | **~580K/s** | **"nearly six hundred thousand metadata writes a second"** | **THE decision: shard the metadata DB** |
| Metadata storage | 500M users x 5,000 files x 1 KB/row x 3 repl x 1.5 index | ~11 PB | "about eleven petabytes of metadata" | sharded SQL / wide-column, ~40+ shards |
| Blob storage | 500M x 15 GB, −30% global dedup, x1.25 versions | ~6,500 PB | "about six and a half exabytes of bytes" | object store (S3-class), tiering |
| Blob storage cost | ~6,500 PB x $0.023/GB-mo | ~$150M/mo | "order of a hundred-fifty million a month in storage" | tiering cold data to cut it |
| **Egress / download cost** | **100M DAU x 0.5 GB/day x 30 x $0.05/GB** | **~$75M/mo** | **"around seventy-five million a month in bandwidth"** | **CDN + dedup + delta-sync to cut egress** |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** peak **metadata writes ≈ 580K/s**, while a single sharded SQL node tops out around **~15K writes/s**. 580K ÷ 15K ≈ **39 → we need ~40+ shards, partitioned by `user_id`.**
- **Flip threshold:** if peak metadata writes drop below ~15K/s (i.e. fewer than ~7.5K change-events/s — a small/internal deployment), a single primary with read replicas is enough and we skip sharding entirely.

Storage bytes are *huge* (exabytes) but not architecturally hard — it's just "buy more object store and tier the cold stuff." The two numbers that actually *force decisions* are the 580K/s metadata writes (→ shard) and the ~$75M/mo egress (→ CDN + dedup + delta sync, our real cost lever).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives the architecture is metadata writes — nearly six hundred thousand a second at peak, against maybe fifteen thousand a second per database node, so I have to shard the metadata DB by user, roughly forty shards. The other big number is money: bandwidth is about seventy-five million dollars a month, more than I'd naively guess, so dedup and delta-sync aren't just nice — they're the cost story. The raw byte storage is enormous but boring; it's just object storage I tier. So I'll spend my design budget on metadata sharding and on never shipping a byte twice, not on the blob store."

---

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the nouns; I'll add full fields in §7 (Data Model).

**User** — owns an account, a set of devices, and a root namespace; the unit I shard by.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "the person whose filing cabinet this is."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you find all of a user's files fast?" → everything is partitioned by `user_id`, so one user's metadata lives on one shard — no cross-shard scan.

**Device** — a registered client (laptop, phone) that holds a **sync cursor** marking how caught-up it is.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a bookmark in the user's change history — 'I've read up to here.'"
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What if a device is offline for a month?" → it presents its old cursor; we hand it everything after it, compacting deletes/overwrites so it doesn't replay a million dead events.

**File (FileMetadata)** — the logical file: name, path, owner, size, current version, and an *ordered list of chunk hashes* — but NOT the bytes.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a recipe card that lists the ingredients (chunks) by name, but isn't the food itself."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Where are the actual bytes?" → in the blob store, addressed by chunk hash; metadata only stores the hash list (the manifest).

**Chunk (Block)** — a content-addressed piece of a file, typically ~4 MB, named by the hash of its contents.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a Lego brick stamped with a fingerprint; identical bricks are stored once and reused everywhere."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you dedup?" → the chunk's name *is* the hash of its bytes, so an identical chunk uploaded by anyone maps to the same object — store once, reference-count it.

**ChangeJournalEntry** — an append-only, per-user ordered log of every metadata mutation (created/modified/deleted/moved), keyed by a monotonically increasing sequence number.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a diary the user's account keeps — devices read forward from their bookmark."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Why a journal and not just diffing?" → diffing 5,000 files on every sync is expensive; reading 'everything after cursor N' is O(changes), not O(files).

**Share (Permission)** — grants a user or link read/write access to a file or folder; the reason a change fans out to multiple journals.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a guest pass pinned to a folder."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How is a shared edit seen by everyone?" → the edit appends an entry to *every* member's change journal (fan-out-on-write for normal shares).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are User, Device, File metadata, Chunk, the per-user Change Journal, and Share. The two I'd flag as the heart of the design are the Chunk — which is content-addressed, meaning its name is literally the hash of its bytes, and that's what makes dedup free — and the Change Journal, which is the append-only diary each user's account keeps so devices can sync by reading forward from a bookmark instead of re-scanning everything. I'll fill in exact fields, primary keys, and shard keys in the data model section."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS. The caller's identity always comes from the JWT (a signed token proving who you are) — we never trust a `user_id` in the body.

### 5.1 Upload — commit a file (FR1)
Upload is two-phase: first ask which chunks are missing, upload only those to the blob store, then **commit** the manifest. POST because it creates new state.

- `path: string` — logical path in the user's namespace (why: the file's identity).
- `chunks: [hash]` — ordered list of chunk hashes (the manifest). Server checks which it already has.
- `baseVersion: int` — the version the client started editing from (why: conflict detection).
- `idempotencyKey: string` — client-generated; dedups retried commits.
- *Absent:* `user_id` (from JWT), `version` (server assigns), `createdAt` (server clock).

```
// 1) Ask what's missing
POST /v1/files/check-chunks
{ "chunks": ["b2a1...", "9f3c...", "b2a1..."] }
// Response 200
{ "missing": ["9f3c..."] }   // b2a1 already in store (dedup hit)

// 2) PUT each missing chunk straight to blob store (presigned URL)
//    PUT https://blob.example/9f3c...   <raw bytes>

// 3) Commit the manifest
POST /v1/files/commit
{ "path": "/work/budget.xlsx", "chunks": ["b2a1...","9f3c...","b2a1..."],
  "baseVersion": 7, "idempotencyKey": "cmt_a91f" }
// Response 200
{ "fileId": "f_8821", "version": 8, "status": "COMMITTED" }
// Response 409 (conflict)
{ "status": "CONFLICT", "serverVersion": 9, "conflictCopyPath": "/work/budget (conflicted copy device-2).xlsx" }
```
- **Key decision:** the `check-chunks` step is what makes dedup and resumability real — we only PUT bytes we lack, and a retried `commit` with the same `idempotencyKey` returns the same version, never a duplicate.

### 5.2 Download a file / chunk (FR2)
GET — read-only.
```
GET /v1/files/f_8821?version=8        -> { "chunks": ["b2a1...","9f3c...","b2a1..."], "size": 10485760 }
GET /v1/chunks/9f3c...                 -> 302 redirect to CDN/presigned blob URL
```
- **Key decision:** chunk fetches redirect to a CDN/presigned URL so bytes flow edge→client and never through our app servers — this is the egress-cost lever.

### 5.3 Sync — get changes since a cursor (FR3)
GET with long-poll. The server holds the request open until there's something past the cursor (or a timeout).
```
GET /v1/sync?cursor=44210&wait=30      // long-poll up to 30s
// Response 200
{ "changes": [
    { "seq": 44211, "fileId": "f_8821", "op": "MODIFIED", "version": 8 },
    { "seq": 44212, "fileId": "f_5510", "op": "DELETED" } ],
  "cursor": 44212 }
```
- **Key decision:** the cursor is server-authoritative and monotonic; the client stores it. Reconnect re-sends the cursor, so delivery is at-least-once and idempotent (re-applying "file is now v8" is harmless).

### 5.4 Share a file or folder (FR4)
POST — creates a permission.
```
POST /v1/shares
{ "fileId": "f_8821", "granteeEmail": "[email protected]", "role": "WRITE" }
// Response 200
{ "shareId": "sh_771", "fileId": "f_8821", "granteeUserId": "u_99", "role": "WRITE" }
```
- **Key decision:** the granter is taken from the JWT and must own (or have admin on) the resource — checked server-side. We never trust a client-sent `ownerId`.

### 5.5 Version history & restore (FR5)
GET list, POST restore (restore creates a new version, so POST not PUT).
```
GET  /v1/files/f_8821/versions   -> { "versions": [ {"version":8,"ts":"...","size":...}, {"version":7,...} ] }
POST /v1/files/f_8821/restore    { "toVersion": 7 }   -> { "version": 9, "restoredFrom": 7 }
```
- **Key decision:** restore is non-destructive — it makes v9 whose chunks equal v7's, so history is never rewritten and the chunks are reused (no re-upload).

> 💬 "Notice the price of every upload is paid in *hashes*, not bytes — we negotiate which chunks are missing before a single byte moves."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The API mirrors the flows: commit a file, download chunks, long-poll for changes, share, and list/restore versions. The one I'd highlight is upload — it's deliberately a three-step dance: I send the list of chunk hashes, the server tells me which it's missing, I upload only those straight to blob storage via a presigned URL, then I commit the manifest with an idempotency key. That gives me dedup, resumability, and safe retries in one shape. And security-wise, the caller is always the JWT — I never trust a user id or owner id from the request body."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)

Shared palette: clients (blue), our services (green), async/queues (amber), datastores as cylinders (grey), external (purple).

### 6.1 Upload a file (chunked, resumable, dedup)
A client splits the file, learns which chunks are missing, uploads only those, then commits a manifest.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Client chunker | Code on the device that cuts a file into ~4 MB content-defined chunks and hashes each | Lets us dedup and resume; only changed chunks move | Not fixed-size offset chunks (one inserted byte shifts every later chunk → re-upload whole file) | Content-defined chunking is CPU work on the client |
| Metadata Service | Our API tier; validates, talks to metadata DB, writes the change journal | Owns the manifest + the per-user ordering | Not letting clients write the DB directly (no auth, no ordering) | Extra hop |
| Block/Blob Store | Object store (S3-class) holding chunks by hash | Cheap, 11-nines durable, scales to exabytes | Not storing blobs in the DB (DBs are terrible at exabytes of binary) | Eventual within object store; manage refcounts ourselves |
| Chunk-index (KV) | Key-value map: chunk-hash → exists? + refcount | O(1) "do we have this chunk?" dedup check | Not scanning the blob store per chunk (way too slow) | Must keep refcount in sync with blob lifecycle |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Content-defined chunking (CDC)** — cutting a file at boundaries chosen by a rolling hash of the content, not at fixed byte offsets. Here it means inserting a byte near the start only re-chunks that region, so we re-upload kilobytes, not the whole file.
> **Content-addressed storage** — naming each chunk by the hash of its own bytes. Here it gives us free dedup: identical content always lands on the same key.
> **Object store** — a service that stores opaque blobs by key with huge durability and capacity. Here it holds the actual file bytes, separate from metadata.
> **Refcount** — a counter of how many files point at a chunk. Here it tells us when a chunk is safe to garbage-collect.

**Upload flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Client chunker]
    C -->|"1 check missing chunks"| MS[Metadata Service]
    C -.->|"3 PUT missing chunks"| BS[(Block Store)]
    subgraph Control["Control plane"]
        MS -->|"2 lookup hashes"| CI[(Chunk index KV)]
        MS -->|"5 write manifest"| MDB[(Metadata DB)]
        MS -->|"6 append change"| CJ[(Change Journal)]
    end
    BS -.->|"4 confirm stored"| CI
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class C client
    class MS svc
    class BS,CI,MDB,CJ store
```
↳ new components: Chunk index, Block Store, Change Journal.

**Numbered narration**
1. **Check missing chunks** — client sends the manifest hashes; Metadata Service queries the Chunk index. *Why here:* the service owns dedup logic. *State:* none yet. *What could go wrong:* index says "have it" but blob was GC'd — we verify presence before relying on a hit.
2. **Lookup hashes** — Chunk index returns which hashes already exist. *State:* none. *Risk:* stale refcount; we re-validate on commit.
3. **PUT missing chunks** — client uploads only missing chunks directly to the Block Store via presigned URLs (resumable, parallel). *State:* new chunks durable. *Risk:* connection drops → resume the unfinished chunk only.
4. **Confirm stored** — Block Store write triggers an index update (exists=true, refcount bump on commit). *State:* chunk known. *Risk:* PUT succeeded but confirm lost → idempotent, hash is the same.
5. **Write manifest** — on commit, Metadata Service writes the FileMetadata row (path, ordered chunk list, new version). *State:* file now points at chunks. *Risk:* `baseVersion` stale → 409 conflict path (§6.5).
6. **Append change** — service appends a ChangeJournalEntry for the user. *State:* sync cursor frontier advances. *Risk:* crash between 5 and 6 → both in one DB transaction (same shard), so atomic.

> 💬 "We never upload a byte we already have — the upload is a negotiation in hashes first, bytes second."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "On upload, the client chops the file into content-defined chunks — boundaries chosen by the content itself, so a small edit only re-chunks a small region — and hashes each one. It asks the server which hashes are missing, uploads just those straight to the object store, then commits a manifest. The manifest write and the change-journal append happen in one transaction on the user's shard, so they're atomic. The trade-off is the client does chunking CPU work and we maintain refcounts ourselves, but in exchange we get dedup, resumability, and we never re-ship known bytes."

### 6.2 Download / sync a file to a device (FR2)
A device pulls the new manifest, fetches only chunks it lacks, reassembles the file.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN | Edge cache near the user serving chunks | Cuts download latency and our egress bill | Not serving every byte from origin (egress cost + latency) | Cache eviction; cold chunks miss to origin |
| Local chunk cache | Chunks already on the device | Skip downloading chunks the device already has | n/a | Disk on device |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a fleet of edge servers caching bytes close to users. Here it serves popular/shared chunks from the edge so we don't pay origin egress or latency repeatedly.

**Sync-down flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Device]
    D -->|"1 get manifest"| MS[Metadata Service]
    MS -->|"2 read FileMetadata"| MDB[(Metadata DB)]
    D -->|"3 fetch missing chunks"| CDN[CDN edge]
    CDN -->|"4 miss to origin"| BS[(Block Store)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2350,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
    class D client
    class MS svc
    class MDB,BS store
    class CDN ext
```
↳ reuses existing: Metadata Service, Metadata DB, Block Store (from §6.1).

**Numbered narration**
1. **Get manifest** — device asks for the file's current chunk list. *Why:* it needs to know which chunks make up v8. *State:* none. *Risk:* version moved again → device gets the latest and re-syncs.
2. **Read FileMetadata** — service reads the manifest from the (possibly replica) Metadata DB. *State:* none. *Risk:* replica lag → for the device's own writes we read from primary (read-your-own-writes, NFR4).
3. **Fetch missing chunks** — device diffs the manifest against its local cache and requests only unseen chunks from the CDN. *State:* file partially reassembled. *Risk:* chunk evicted at edge → step 4.
4. **Miss to origin** — CDN misses fall through to the Block Store, then cache. *State:* chunk warm at edge. *Risk:* origin slow → retries; durability unaffected.

> 💬 "Download is the mirror of upload — diff manifests, pull only the deltas, and let the CDN eat the repeat reads."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Download is upload in reverse. The device gets the new manifest, compares it to the chunks it already has locally, and pulls only the missing ones — and those come from a CDN edge so we're not paying origin egress on every read. The one correctness nuance is read-your-own-writes: for a device reading back a file it just uploaded, I route that read to the primary, not a lagging replica, so it never sees a stale list."

### 6.3 Detect & propagate changes — the sync engine (FR3)
This is the engine behind FR2: how a device learns *something* changed without polling the DB.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Notification Service | Holds long-poll/WebSocket connections; pushes "you have changes" | Fan-out wakeups cheaply to 300M devices | Not clients polling every 5s (≈60M req/s, melts the DB) | A big stateful connection fleet (~3,000 boxes) |
| Change Journal | Append-only per-user ordered log of mutations + cursor | Sync = "read entries after cursor N", O(changes) not O(files) | Not diffing full file lists each sync (5,000-file scan per pull) | Journal storage + compaction |
| Pub/Sub bus | Async fabric routing a "user X changed" signal to the right notify box | Decouples writers from the connection fleet | Not having Metadata Service call every notify box directly | Extra hop; at-least-once delivery |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Long-poll** — the client makes a request the server holds open until there's news (or ~30s passes). Here it gives near-real-time wakeups without a constant socket per device.
> **Change Journal + cursor** — an append-only log of a user's mutations plus a per-device bookmark. Here it makes catch-up cheap and idempotent.
> **Pub/Sub** — a message bus where publishers emit events and subscribers receive them. Here it carries "user X has new changes" from the writer to whichever notify box holds that user's devices.

**Sync engine flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Writer device]
    W -->|"1 commit"| MS[Metadata Service]
    subgraph WritePath["Write path"]
        MS -->|"2 append entry"| CJ[(Change Journal)]
        MS -.->|"3 publish user changed"| PS[[Pub Sub bus]]
    end
    subgraph NotifyPath["Notify path"]
        PS -.->|"4 route to box"| NS[Notification Service]
        NS -->|"5 wake long-poll"| RD[Reader device]
    end
    RD -->|"6 get changes after cursor"| MS
    MS -->|"7 read entries"| CJ
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class W,RD client
    class MS,NS svc
    class PS async
    class CJ store
```
↳ new components: Notification Service, Pub/Sub bus. Reuses: Metadata Service, Change Journal.

**Numbered narration**
1. **Commit** — writer device commits a change (from §6.1). *State:* manifest written.
2. **Append entry** — service appends a journal entry with the next sequence number for that user. *Why:* gives a total order per user. *State:* cursor frontier moves. *Risk:* same transaction as the manifest, so atomic.
3. **Publish user-changed** — service emits a lightweight "user X changed" event onto the Pub/Sub bus. *State:* none durable. *Risk:* publish lost → device's next long-poll timeout re-checks anyway (safety net).
4. **Route to box** — bus routes to the Notification Service box that holds user X's open connections. *Risk:* device on a different box → consistent hashing on `user_id` maps user→box.
5. **Wake long-poll** — notify box completes the held long-poll for the reader's devices with "go sync". *State:* device knows to pull. *Risk:* device offline → it'll catch up on reconnect via its cursor.
6. **Get changes after cursor** — reader calls `/sync?cursor=N`. *State:* none yet.
7. **Read entries** — service returns journal entries after N and the new cursor. *State:* device advances its bookmark. *Risk:* duplicate delivery → re-applying is idempotent.

> 💬 "Polling 300 million devices would be 60 million requests a second; instead each device sleeps on a long-poll and we *wake* the ones that have news."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The sync engine is the cleverest part. Instead of devices polling, each one parks on a long-poll. When someone commits a change, we append it to that user's change journal — an append-only log with a sequence number — and publish a tiny 'user X changed' event. A pub/sub bus routes it to the one notification box holding that user's devices, which wakes their long-polls. They then call sync with their cursor and read only the entries after it. The journal's the key trick: catch-up is reading forward from a bookmark, which is cheap and idempotent on retry. If a notification is ever lost, the long-poll timeout is the safety net — worst case sync is a few seconds later, not broken."

### 6.4 Share a file or folder (FR4)
A share puts one file/folder into multiple users' worlds; an edit must reach all of them.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Share/Permission store | Table mapping (resource → grantees, role) | Cheap authz check on every read/commit | Not embedding ACLs in each file row (folder shares would duplicate massively) | Extra lookup per access |
| Fan-out worker | Async worker that, on a shared-resource change, appends to each member's journal | Keeps the share edit fast; fan-out happens off the hot path | Not fanning out synchronously in the commit (slow, couples members) | Brief delay before all members see it |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **ACL (Access Control List)** — the list of who can read/write a resource. Here it's checked server-side on every access using the JWT identity.
> **Fan-out-on-write** — when a change happens, immediately copy a pointer into each interested party's feed/journal. Here it puts a shared edit into every member's change journal so their normal sync picks it up.

**Share fan-out flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Member device]
    U -->|"1 commit shared file"| MS[Metadata Service]
    MS -->|"2 check permission"| PERM[(Share store)]
    MS -->|"3 write manifest plus own journal"| CJ[(Change Journal)]
    MS -.->|"4 enqueue fan-out"| Q[[Fan-out queue]]
    Q -.->|"5 consume"| FW[Fan-out worker]
    FW -->|"6 append to each member journal"| CJ
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class U client
    class MS,FW svc
    class Q async
    class PERM,CJ store
```
↳ new components: Share store, Fan-out queue + worker. Reuses: Metadata Service, Change Journal.

**Numbered narration**
1. **Commit shared file** — a member edits a file in a shared folder. *State:* pending.
2. **Check permission** — service verifies the JWT user has WRITE on the resource via the Share store. *Risk:* no permission → 403.
3. **Write manifest + own journal** — manifest written; the committer's own journal entry appended synchronously. *State:* committer sees it immediately (read-your-own-writes).
4. **Enqueue fan-out** — a job to notify other members is enqueued. *Why async:* the commit shouldn't wait on N members. *Risk:* queue backed up → members lag a few seconds, eventually consistent.
5. **Consume** — fan-out worker picks up the job. *Risk:* worker crash → at-least-once redelivery; journal appends are idempotent by (resource, version).
6. **Append to each member journal** — worker appends an entry to each grantee's journal, waking their syncs. *State:* all members converge. *Risk:* huge share (millions) → switch to fan-out-on-read (deep dive §8).

> 💬 "A normal share fans the change out into each member's diary; only a celebrity-sized share flips to fan-out-on-read."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Sharing means a file now lives in several users' journals. When a member edits a shared file, I check the permission, write the manifest, and append to the committer's own journal synchronously so they see it instantly. Then I enqueue an async fan-out job that appends an entry to every other member's journal, which wakes their sync. I keep fan-out off the hot path so the commit stays fast, accepting that other members see it a couple seconds later. For a folder shared with millions I'd flip to fan-out-on-read, which I'll cover in the deep dives."

### 6.5 Version history & conflict resolution (FR5)
Every commit makes a new version; concurrent offline edits create a conflicted copy.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Version chain | Each file keeps an ordered list of versions, each a manifest pointing at chunks | History + restore are just older manifests; chunks reused | Not storing full copies per version (exabytes wasted) | Old chunks pinned until retention expires |
| Conflict detector | Compares the commit's `baseVersion` to the server's current version | Deterministic detection of concurrent edits | Not last-writer-wins silently (loses data) | A "conflicted copy" file appears |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Version vector / baseVersion** — the version a client started from. Here, if it doesn't match the server's current version, two people edited concurrently and we branch instead of overwrite.
> **Conflicted copy** — a second file we create holding the loser's edit. Here it's our never-lose-data policy for offline multi-edit.

**Conflict flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device A commit base 7]
    B[Device B commit base 7]
    A -->|"1 commit"| MS[Metadata Service]
    B -->|"2 commit later"| MS
    MS -->|"3 A matches base accept v8"| MDB[(Metadata DB)]
    MS -->|"4 B base stale make conflict copy"| MDB
    MS -.->|"5 journal both"| CJ[(Change Journal)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class A,B client
    class MS svc
    class MDB,CJ store
```
↳ reuses existing: Metadata Service, Metadata DB, Change Journal.

**Numbered narration**
1. **A commits** — Device A commits with `baseVersion 7`; server's current is 7 → accept as v8. *State:* file is v8.
2. **B commits later** — Device B (offline) commits also with `baseVersion 7`. *Risk:* it doesn't know about v8.
3. **A accepted** — A's commit is the clean fast-forward. *State:* v8 authoritative.
4. **B is stale → conflict copy** — server sees B's base (7) ≠ current (8), so it creates `budget (conflicted copy device-B).xlsx` as a sibling. *State:* both edits preserved. *Risk:* none lost — that's the whole point.
5. **Journal both** — both the v8 update and the new conflicted-copy file are journaled, so all devices see both. *State:* everyone converges, user decides what to keep.

> 💬 "We never silently pick a winner — if two devices both branched from version 7, we keep both and let the human merge."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Versioning is cheap because a version is just an older manifest pointing at chunks we already store — restore is non-destructive and reuses chunks. The hard case is two offline edits. Each commit carries the baseVersion it started from. The first to land fast-forwards cleanly. The second, whose base is now stale, doesn't overwrite — we create a clearly-named conflicted copy so no edit is ever lost. Both land in the journal so every device sees both, and the user resolves it. It's deterministic and it never destroys data, which is the only acceptable policy for someone's files."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Stepping back: there are clearly two planes. The **control plane** — Metadata Service, the sharded metadata DB, change journal, share store, and chunk index — owns the *truth about files* and is small, structured, strongly-ordered per user. The **data plane** — Block Store and CDN — owns the *bytes*, is enormous, and is cheap and eventually consistent. The **async plane** — pub/sub plus the fan-out queue — connects them by waking devices and propagating shared changes. Every hard problem lives in the control plane and in keeping the two planes' refcounts honest."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

### PART A — Entity table

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key noted) | Chosen store | Partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | **user_id (PK, shard key)**, email, home_region | Sharded SQL | user_id | Strong within shard |
| Device | **device_id (PK)**, user_id (shard key), cursor (seq) | Sharded SQL | user_id | Strong within shard |
| FileMetadata | **file_id (PK)**, user_id (shard key), path, size, current_version, chunk_list (hashes) | Sharded SQL | user_id | Strong within shard |
| FileVersion | **(file_id, version) (PK)**, chunk_list, created_at, author_device | Sharded SQL | user_id | Strong within shard |
| Chunk | **chunk_hash (PK)**, size, refcount, blob_location | Chunk index (KV) + Block Store | chunk_hash | Eventual (object store) |
| ChangeJournalEntry | **(user_id, seq) (PK, seq monotonic)**, file_id, op, version, ts | Sharded SQL / log | user_id | Strong, ordered per user |
| Share | **share_id (PK)**, resource_id, owner_id, grantee_id, role | Sharded SQL | resource_owner | Strong within shard |

### PART B — Storage decision cards

**🗄️ Sharded SQL (Postgres/Vitess-style) — used for: User, Device, FileMetadata, FileVersion, ChangeJournal, Share**
1. **What it is:** a relational database split across ~40 nodes by `user_id`; each node holds the full file metadata for a slice of users with ACID transactions.
2. **Why — the access pattern that forces it:** a commit must atomically write the manifest *and* append the ordered change-journal entry. Only a transactional store gives me "both or neither" plus a per-user monotonic sequence. And because everything is keyed by `user_id`, one user's data lives on one shard — no cross-shard joins on the hot path.
3. **Considered & rejected:**
   - **Single Postgres primary** — caps near ~15K writes/s; we peak at ~580K/s. Doesn't fit.
   - **DynamoDB (pure KV)** — great scaling, but per-user monotonic sequencing and multi-row atomic commits are awkward; I'd reinvent transactions in app code.
   - **MongoDB** — flexible, but I want battle-tested per-shard ACID for the manifest+journal atomic write.
4. **Trade-off:** manual resharding and no cross-user joins (sharing needs the explicit Share store + fan-out). Acceptable — shares are the exception, not the hot path.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Metadata goes in a SQL database sharded by user id, about forty shards, because the commit has to atomically write the file row and append an ordered change-journal entry — that's a transaction, and per-user sequencing. A single Postgres tops out around fifteen thousand writes a second and I'm at nearly six hundred thousand, so sharding isn't optional."

**🗄️ Block Store (S3-class object store) — used for: Chunk bytes**
1. **What it is:** a managed object store holding opaque blobs by key with eleven-nines durability and effectively unbounded capacity.
2. **Why — the access pattern:** I have ~6,500 PB of immutable, content-addressed chunks. I need cheap, durable, write-once/read-many storage — exactly what object stores are for, and the key *is* the chunk hash so dedup is automatic.
3. **Considered & rejected:**
   - **Blobs in the DB** — DBs choke on petabytes of binary; bloats backups and kills cache hit rates.
   - **Self-hosted file servers** — we'd reinvent durability/replication/erasure coding; not worth it at this bar.
4. **Trade-off:** eventual consistency and we own refcounting/garbage-collection of unreferenced chunks.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The actual bytes live in an S3-class object store, addressed by content hash, so identical chunks dedup for free and we get eleven-nines durability without building it. The cost is we manage refcounts ourselves to know when a chunk is safe to delete."

**🗄️ Chunk index (KV / Redis+durable KV) — used for: chunk-hash → exists/refcount/location**
1. **What it is:** a fast key-value store mapping a chunk hash to "do we have it, how many files use it, where is it."
2. **Why — the access pattern:** the dedup check is a point lookup by hash on every chunk of every upload — ~290K/s peak. That's a KV workload, not a relational one.
3. **Considered & rejected:**
   - **A column in the SQL DB** — adds load to the shard that's already the bottleneck; the hash space doesn't shard by user.
   - **Scanning the object store** — far too slow for an inline dedup check.
4. **Trade-off:** another store to keep consistent with the blob lifecycle (refcount drift handled by a periodic reconciliation job).
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Dedup needs an O(1) 'do we already have this hash?' lookup on every chunk, hundreds of thousands a second, so that's a key-value store keyed by chunk hash, separate from the per-user metadata shards."

**🗄️ CDN — used for: serving chunk bytes to devices**
1. **What it is:** edge caches near users serving popular/shared chunks.
2. **Why:** egress is ~$75M/mo; caching repeat reads (shared files, recently synced) at the edge cuts both latency and origin egress.
3. **Considered & rejected:** origin-only serving (latency + the full egress bill).
4. **Trade-off:** cold/unique chunks miss to origin; cache offers no benefit for one-reader files.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Downloads go through a CDN so shared and repeat reads hit the edge — that's the main lever on a seventy-five-million-a-month bandwidth bill."

### PART C — Per-operation consistency

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commit manifest + journal append | Sharded SQL | Strong (single-shard txn) | Atomicity + per-user ordering are mandatory |
| Device reads its own just-uploaded file | Sharded SQL primary | Read-your-own-writes | NFR4 — must not look like the upload failed |
| Another device / member sees a change | Journal + pub/sub | Eventual (~seconds) | NFR2 allows ~10s; cheap fan-out |
| Chunk fetch | Block Store / CDN | Eventual | Chunks are immutable by hash, so staleness is impossible |
| Dedup lookup | Chunk index KV | Eventual w/ verify-on-commit | A false "have it" is caught at commit; a false "missing" just re-uploads |
| Cross-region file list | Replica | Eventual | NFR5 — far region may lag a bit; home shard is source of truth |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage splits cleanly. Metadata — files, versions, the change journal, shares — lives in SQL sharded by user id, because a commit is an atomic transaction that also has to advance a per-user sequence. The bytes live in an object store addressed by content hash, which gives dedup and eleven-nines durability for free. A small key-value store holds the chunk dedup index because that lookup is hundreds of thousands a second. And a CDN fronts downloads to control egress cost. I considered putting everything in DynamoDB, but I rejected it because the atomic manifest-plus-journal write and per-user ordering are exactly what a transactional store is good at and a pure KV store would make me rebuild."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two enemies — re-shipping known bytes, and telling 300M devices what changed — and reason forward from there.

### How do we avoid re-uploading bytes we already have? (chunking + dedup)

#### Bad: whole-file upload, hash the whole file
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Hash the entire file; if the hash changed, upload the entire file again."
**Approach:** compute one SHA-256 over the file. If it differs from what's stored, PUT the whole file.
**Why people try this:** it's the obvious, simplest thing.
**⚠️ What breaks:** edit one line of a 1 GB file and you re-upload 1 GB. At ~290K changes/s averaging 2 MB, that's fine — but for large files it destroys NFR5 and blows ingress/egress. No resumability either: a drop at 90% restarts at 0%.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any file over a few MB edited frequently makes whole-file upload unusable.

#### Good: fixed-size chunking + content-addressing
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** whole-file re-upload moves gigabytes for a one-byte edit.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Cut the file into equal 4 MB pieces, name each by its hash, upload only pieces whose hash we don't have."
**Approach:** split at fixed 4 MB offsets, hash each chunk, store by hash (content-addressed). Upload only missing hashes; dedup is automatic since identical chunks share a key.
**⚠️ What breaks:** the **boundary-shift problem** — insert one byte at the *start* and every subsequent fixed-offset chunk shifts, so all hashes change and you re-upload the whole file anyway. Fixed chunking only dedups appends and exact-match files.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** prepends/inserts (very common: log files, prepended headers, edited docs) defeat fixed chunking.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[File bytes]
    F -->|"cut at 4MB offsets"| K1[chunk 1 hash a]
    F --> K2[chunk 2 hash b]
    F --> K3[chunk 3 hash c]
    K1 -->|"check index"| CI[(Chunk index)]
    K2 --> CI
    K3 --> CI
    CI -->|"upload only missing"| BS[(Block Store)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class F svc
    class K1,K2,K3 svc
    class CI,BS store
```

#### Great: content-defined chunking (CDC) + refcounted dedup + resumable upload
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** fixed offsets shift on insert, so a prepend re-uploads everything.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Choose the cut points based on the content itself, using a rolling fingerprint, so inserting bytes only re-chunks the area around the change."
**Approach:** run a rolling hash (Rabin fingerprint — a cheap hash over a sliding window) across the file; cut a boundary whenever the fingerprint hits a target pattern (giving ~4 MB average). Because boundaries follow content, an insert shifts only nearby boundaries — the rest of the chunks keep their hashes and dedup. Each chunk is content-addressed; the Chunk index keeps a **refcount** so we only garbage-collect a chunk when no file references it. Upload is resumable: the client checks-then-PUTs missing chunks in parallel and a dropped connection only retries the unfinished chunk.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** dedup hit ratio ~40% → at 290K chunk-refs/s peak we do only ~174K actual blob writes/s. Across the fleet, 40% fewer blob writes and a big cut in ingress. On a 1 GB file with a 10 KB edit: ~3 changed chunks (~12 MB) move instead of 1 GB — ~99% saved.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Connection drops mid-upload | Resume: re-PUT only the in-flight chunk (content-addressed, idempotent) |
| Index says "have it" but blob GC'd | Verify-on-commit; if missing, request the chunk and upload |
| Two users upload identical chunk | Same hash → stored once, refcount = 2 |
| Hash collision (different bytes, same hash) | SHA-256 collision is astronomically unlikely; verify-on-write byte-compare on collision-detect |
| Chunk's last referencing file deleted | Refcount → 0; async GC removes blob after retention window |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The clean answer is content-defined chunking. I cut the file at boundaries chosen by a rolling fingerprint of the content, not at fixed offsets, so inserting bytes only re-chunks the region around the edit — everything else keeps its hash and dedups. Each chunk is named by its hash and reference-counted, so identical content is stored once and only garbage-collected when nothing points at it. With about a forty percent dedup hit, peak blob writes drop from two-ninety thousand a second to about a hundred seventy-five thousand, and a tiny edit to a gigabyte file moves megabytes, not gigabytes."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just delta-sync against the previous version?":** "We do — that's exactly what CDC gives us, a delta in chunk units. True byte-delta (rsync-style) is even tighter but needs the old version present to diff against; CDC dedups globally across all files and users, which delta-against-previous can't."

### How do we tell 300M devices what changed without melting the DB? (sync engine)

#### Bad: clients poll the DB every few seconds
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Every device asks the database 'anything new?' every few seconds."
**Approach:** each device polls `/sync` on a 5s timer.
**⚠️ What breaks:** 300M devices ÷ 5s = **~60M requests/s** of mostly-empty "nope" responses hammering the metadata shards. The shards die; 99% of the traffic is wasted.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the poll QPS alone exceeds any DB by 100x.

#### Good: long-poll + per-user change journal
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** constant polling produces 60M req/s of empty answers.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Devices ask once and the server *holds* the question open until there's actually news."
**Approach:** device calls `/sync?cursor=N&wait=30`; the server holds the connection until a new journal entry passes N or ~30s elapses. The Change Journal makes the answer O(changes-after-N), not a full file scan. On a change, a pub/sub event wakes the held connection.
**⚠️ What breaks:** holding 300M connections still needs ~3,000 notify boxes, and a naive design re-reads the DB on every wake. Also, which box holds a given user's connection? Routing matters.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** at 300M connections you need deterministic user→box routing and you must not thunder-herd the DB when a popular shared file changes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Device]
```

#### Great: consistent-hash routing + cursor + journal compaction + WS upgrade
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** undefined box routing and DB thundering on hot changes.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Map each user to one notification box by hashing their id, keep a per-device bookmark, and squash dead history so a long-offline device catches up cheaply."
**Approach:** route `user_id` to a notify box via consistent hashing (a scheme where adding/removing boxes only remaps a small slice of users). The cursor is server-authoritative and monotonic. **Journal compaction:** if a file was modified 50 times since a device's cursor, catch-up only needs the latest state, so we collapse runs into "current state of file F" — a device offline a month replays state, not every event. Foreground apps upgrade to a WebSocket for sub-second pushes; background uses long-poll.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** polling = 300M ÷ 5s = ~60M req/s (impossible). Long-poll = a wake only on actual change ≈ peak ~290K wakes/s spread over ~3,000 boxes ≈ ~100/s/box. That's a ~200x reduction.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Notify box dies | Devices reconnect; consistent hashing remaps them; they re-present cursors — no lost changes |
| Pub/sub event lost | Long-poll ~30s timeout re-checks the journal — the safety net |
| Device offline a month | Presents old cursor; compacted catch-up returns current state, not full history |
| Duplicate wake / delivery | Re-applying "file F is now v8" is idempotent |
| Hot shared file (many members) | Fan-out is async + batched; members converge in seconds |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Polling would be sixty million requests a second of mostly 'nothing new' — a non-starter. So devices long-poll: they ask once and we hold it open until their change journal advances past their cursor or thirty seconds pass. Each user maps to one notification box by consistent hashing, so a pub/sub 'user changed' event finds the right box and wakes only the affected long-polls — about a couple hundred a second per box instead of melting the DB. The cursor makes catch-up idempotent, and I compact the journal so a device that's been offline a month replays current state, not a million dead edits. The long-poll timeout is the safety net if a notification is ever lost."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not Kafka per user?":** "Per-user Kafka topics at 500M users is topic explosion. The journal lives in the user's existing metadata shard, ordered by sequence number; pub/sub only carries a tiny 'user X changed' wake signal, not the data."

### How do we resolve concurrent edits without losing data? (conflict resolution)

#### Bad: last-writer-wins by timestamp
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Whoever saved last wins; the other edit is discarded."
**⚠️ What breaks:** clocks drift across devices, so 'last' is wrong; and a user *silently loses work* — the cardinal sin for a file service. NFR durability of *intent* is violated even if bytes are safe.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any real offline-multi-edit case loses data.

#### Good: optimistic concurrency with baseVersion
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** silent data loss.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Each save says which version it started from; if the server moved on, reject it."
**Approach:** commit carries `baseVersion`; if it ≠ server's current, return 409. Client must re-sync and retry.
**⚠️ What breaks:** correct but *user-hostile* — the offline device that just got back online is told "rejected, you figure it out," and the edit is stuck locally with no clear resolution.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** we need a never-lose-data *and* user-friendly outcome, automatically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Device commit base 7]
    C -->|"commit"| MS[Metadata Service]
    MS -->|"compare base vs current"| MDB[(Metadata DB)]
    MDB -->|"match accept"| OK[v8 committed]
    MDB -->|"mismatch reject"| NO[409 resync]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class C client
    class MS,OK,NO svc
    class MDB store
```

#### Great: version vectors + automatic conflicted copies
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** plain 409 dumps resolution on the user and can't tell "concurrent" from "stale."
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Track per-device edit history so we can tell true concurrent edits apart, and when they clash, keep both as separate files automatically."
**Approach:** each file carries a **version vector** (a per-device counter map, e.g. {A:3, B:1}). On commit, compare vectors: if one *dominates* the other (strictly newer), fast-forward. If they're *concurrent* (neither dominates — both edited from a common ancestor), we detect a true conflict and create a **conflicted copy** — a sibling file `name (conflicted copy device-B).ext` holding the loser's chunks. Both are journaled so every device sees both; the human merges. No clock dependence, no data loss, no manual 409 dance.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** with ~3 devices/user and ~50 changes/day, true offline-concurrent edits on the *same* file are rare (well under 0.1% of commits), so conflicted copies are infrequent — cheap to handle correctly, unacceptable to handle wrong.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| A then B sequentially (B saw A's v8) | B's vector dominates → clean fast-forward to v9 |
| A and B both from v7 offline | Concurrent (neither dominates) → conflicted copy, both kept |
| Three-way concurrent edit | N−1 conflicted copies; all preserved |
| Commit retried after success | Idempotency key returns same version, no new copy |
| Conflicted copy itself edited | Treated as an independent file with its own vector |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Last-writer-wins is unacceptable — it loses someone's work and relies on clocks. I use version vectors: each file tracks a per-device edit counter, so I can tell whether one edit is strictly newer than another or whether they're genuinely concurrent — both branched from the same ancestor. If one dominates, I fast-forward. If they're concurrent, I don't pick a winner; I create a clearly-labeled conflicted copy holding the loser's version and journal both, so every device sees both and the user merges. It's deterministic, clock-independent, and never loses data — which is the only acceptable bar for files."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not auto-merge like Google Docs?":** "Character-level merge needs operational transforms or CRDTs and only works for known formats like text. We sync opaque files — a binary or a spreadsheet can't be safely auto-merged, so a conflicted copy is the honest, lossless choice."

### How do we scale metadata and handle hot shares? (metadata scaling + celebrity)

#### Good: shard metadata by user_id
**🗣️ Plain words:** "Split the database by user so each box handles a slice of users."
**Approach:** partition all metadata by `user_id` across ~40 shards; a user's files, versions, journal, and devices all co-locate, so commits are single-shard transactions.
**⚠️ What breaks:** a **folder shared with millions** lives in millions of journals — fan-out-on-write would append millions of entries per edit, a write storm into many shards. Also a single user with a massive account can hot-spot one shard.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** viral public shares break uniform fan-out.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Router by user id]
    R -->|"hash user"| S1[(Shard 1)]
    R --> S2[(Shard 2)]
    R --> S3[(Shard 40)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class R svc
    class S1,S2,S3 store
```

#### Great: hybrid fan-out (write for small shares, read for hot shares)
**↩️ Previous got wrong:** fan-out-on-write to millions is a write storm.
**🗣️ Plain words:** "For normal shares, copy the change into each member's diary. For a giant share, don't copy — let members check the shared folder's own diary when they sync."
**Approach:** keep a per-resource change journal for shared folders. **Small shares (< ~1,000 members):** fan-out-on-write — append to each member's journal (instant, simple). **Hot shares (≥ threshold):** flip to **fan-out-on-read** — members' sync also reads the *shared resource's* journal and merges it with their own by cursor, so one edit is O(1) writes regardless of member count. A flag on the Share marks which mode. This is the classic celebrity/feed hybrid applied to shared folders.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** a folder shared with 1M members edited once = 1M journal appends under fan-out-on-write — a spike across many shards. Fan-out-on-read = 1 append + members pull on their own cadence. Threshold flip ≈ where member-count × edit-rate exceeds the per-shard write budget.
**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Normal team folder edit | Fan-out-on-write; members see it in seconds |
| Viral 1M-member folder edit | Fan-out-on-read; one write, members merge on sync |
| Member joins a hot share | Their sync starts reading the resource journal from join cursor |
| Hot user single-account hot-spot | Sub-shard by (user_id, file_id range) if one user is enormous |
| Shard rebalance | Consistent hashing remaps a slice; journals move with the user |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Metadata shards by user id, about forty shards, so every commit is a single-shard transaction with per-user ordering. The trap is a folder shared with millions — fan-out-on-write would turn one edit into a million journal appends. So I go hybrid: small shares fan out on write for instant convergence, but once a share crosses a member threshold I flip it to fan-out-on-read, where members' sync also reads the shared folder's own journal. One edit becomes one write regardless of audience size. It's the same celebrity-fan-out trick from news feeds, applied to shared folders."

> 🧠 **If they ask "how do you pick the threshold?":** "Where member-count times edit-rate would exceed a shard's write budget — measured, not guessed, and per-share so a hot folder flips independently."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

### 9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean)
- **Data plane (bytes) — 11 nines durability, ~4 nines availability.** "Durability eleven nines = expect to lose under one file in ten million over ten thousand years. Availability four nines = bytes reachable all but ~52 min/year." Mechanism: erasure coding (splitting each chunk into data+parity fragments across many disks/AZs so several can fail with zero loss) + multi-AZ replication + CDN.
- **Control plane (metadata) — 4 nines (99.99%, ~52 min/year).** Mechanism: each shard has a primary + 2 replicas across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centers in one region so one building's power loss is survived by automatic failover); automated leader election.
- **Sync/notify — best-effort with a long-poll safety net.** A lost wake costs ~seconds of extra latency, never a lost change, because the journal + cursor are the source of truth.

### 9B — Per-dependency failure table

| Component | What breaks | Graceful degradation — what the USER SEES | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Metadata shard primary | That user-slice can't write | Their uploads queue locally; reads served from replica; "syncing…" spinner | Replica promoted in seconds; queued commits flush |
| Block Store (region) | Chunk reads/writes fail in region | Reads fail over to replicated region; new uploads buffer on device | Region heals; buffered chunks upload |
| Chunk index KV | Dedup check fails | We *over-upload* (treat all as missing) — slower & costlier but correct | Index rebuilt from blob store metadata |
| Notification Service | No push wakeups | Sync falls back to periodic long-poll/poll — slightly staler (~30s) | Boxes restart; consistent hashing remaps users |
| Pub/Sub bus | Wake events lost | Long-poll timeout re-checks journal; sync still works, just slower | Bus recovers; backlog drains |
| Fan-out worker | Shared edits not propagated | Committer sees own change; members lag | At-least-once redelivery; idempotent appends catch up |
| CDN | Edge misses | Downloads slower (origin egress) but succeed | CDN heals; cache re-warms |

### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class
> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much recent data we can lose if it all crashes right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — the write was durably replicated before we ack'd it.
> **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back after a failure.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| File bytes (chunks) | ≈0 | seconds | Erasure-coded + multi-region; immutable, so no corruption window |
| File metadata + version chain | ≈0 | < 1 min | Synchronous replication within shard before ack; multi-AZ failover |
| Change journal | ≈0 | < 1 min | Same transaction as metadata; co-located, synchronously replicated |
| Chunk index (dedup) | minutes OK | minutes | Rebuildable from blob store; losing it only costs temporary over-upload |
| CDN cache | total loss OK | seconds | Pure cache; re-warms from origin |

### 9D — Cost breakdown
1. **Blob storage — ~$150M/mo.** Why: ~6,500 PB of bytes at object-store rates. Biggest *absolute* line. Optimization: tier cold chunks (untouched > 30d) to cheap cold storage (Glacier-class) — most stored bytes are rarely re-read.
2. **Egress / bandwidth — ~$75M/mo.** Why: 100M users pulling ~0.5 GB/day = ~1,500 PB/mo at egress rates. This is the cost the *architecture* fights. Optimization: CDN + dedup + delta-sync so we never ship a chunk a device already has.
3. **Metadata fleet + notify fleet — order of millions/mo.** Why: ~40 sharded SQL nodes + replicas + ~3,000 notify boxes holding 300M connections. Optimization: long-poll over WebSockets for backgrounded apps (idle connections are cheap), autoscale notify boxes by connection count.

**The single biggest optimization:** aggressive **dedup + delta-sync + cold-tiering** — together they attack both the $75M egress *and* the $150M storage, the two dominant lines. That's why "never ship/store a byte twice" is the design's north star, not a micro-optimization.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a metadata shard primary fails, the user sees a brief 'syncing' spinner — reads come off a replica, writes queue locally, and a replica is promoted in seconds with zero data loss because writes are synchronously replicated before we ack. If the notification fleet hiccups, sync just gets a bit staler because the long-poll timeout still re-checks the journal — nothing is lost. Cost-wise, storage is the biggest absolute bill at around a hundred-fifty million a month and egress about seventy-five, so dedup, delta-sync, and cold-tiering aren't nice-to-haves, they're the cost strategy."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Content-defined chunking vs whole-file (or fixed-block) upload**
1. **Chose & why:** CDC — only changed regions re-upload, dedup works across inserts; directly serves NFR5 and the egress bill.
2. **Gave up:** client CPU to compute the rolling hash, and we own chunk refcounting/GC.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We slice files where the content tells us to, so a small edit moves a small amount, not the whole file."
4. **Reverses when:** if all files were unique, client-side end-to-end encrypted blobs (dedup hit → 0), chunking overhead isn't worth it — store whole encrypted objects.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I chose content-defined chunking because it's the whole reason a one-line edit doesn't cost a gigabyte; the price is client CPU and us managing refcounts."

**🔀 Decision: Long-poll change journal vs client polling (or always-on WebSockets)**
1. **Chose & why:** long-poll + per-user journal — ~200x fewer requests than polling, far cheaper than 300M always-on sockets.
2. **Gave up:** a few seconds of worst-case latency vs true real-time; a stateful notify fleet to manage.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Devices ask once and we hold the question open until there's news, instead of nagging the server constantly."
4. **Reverses when:** if the product needs sub-second sync (live collaboration), upgrade all clients to WebSockets and eat the connection cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Polling would be sixty million requests a second of 'nothing new,' so I long-poll against a per-user change journal; the trade is a few seconds of latency, which my ten-second SLO allows."

**🔀 Decision: Conflicted copies vs last-writer-wins (or auto-merge)**
1. **Chose & why:** version vectors + conflicted copies — never lose data, deterministic, clock-independent.
2. **Gave up:** the user occasionally has to manually merge two copies.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "If two devices edited offline, we keep both and let you decide, rather than silently throwing one away."
4. **Reverses when:** if all content were mergeable text and we scoped in collaborative editing, we'd use CRDTs to auto-merge instead.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For files I will not silently lose an edit, so concurrent offline edits become a clearly-named conflicted copy — last-writer-wins would quietly delete someone's work."

**🔀 Decision: Metadata/blob split (SQL + object store) vs one store for everything**
1. **Chose & why:** structured, ordered, transactional metadata in sharded SQL; immutable bytes in an object store — each store does what it's best at.
2. **Gave up:** two consistency models to reason about and refcount reconciliation between them.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We keep the 'card catalog' in a database and the 'books' in a warehouse, because the catalog needs strict rules and the warehouse just needs to be huge and cheap."
4. **Reverses when:** at tiny scale (a startup), a single Postgres with bytea/blobs is simpler and fine until you hit terabytes.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I split metadata from bytes because the metadata needs transactions and ordering while the bytes just need cheap durable capacity — forcing both into one store would cripple one of them."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd defend hardest: content-defined chunking, because it's the entire reason sync is bandwidth-efficient; long-poll over a change journal, because polling 300 million devices is impossible and always-on sockets are overkill for a ten-second SLO; conflicted copies over last-writer-wins, because losing a file is the one unforgivable failure; and splitting metadata from blobs so each store does its job. Each flips at a clear threshold — sub-second SLO flips me to WebSockets, end-to-end encryption kills dedup, and startup scale collapses everything back into one Postgres."
