## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

### Core / hardest part
**❓ "Two devices edit the same file offline and both come back online — who wins?"**
*Mechanism:* each commit carries a version vector (a per-device edit-counter map). If one strictly dominates, it fast-forwards; if neither dominates, the edits are concurrent and we create a conflicted-copy sibling file holding the loser's chunks, journaling both.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Nobody silently wins — if both edited offline, we keep both as separate files and let you merge."
💬 *One-liner:* "We never resolve a file conflict by deleting data; concurrent edits become a labeled conflicted copy."

**❓ "How does a one-line edit to a 1 GB file upload only kilobytes?"**
*Mechanism:* content-defined chunking cuts boundaries by a rolling content hash, so the edit only re-chunks its local region; unchanged chunks keep their hashes and dedup against what's already stored, so only the changed chunks upload.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "We slice files where the content changes, so we only re-send the slices that actually changed."
💬 *One-liner:* "Content-defined chunking means a small edit moves a few chunks, not the whole file."

**❓ "Walk me through what happens from save to the file appearing on another device."**
*Mechanism:* client chunks+commits → manifest + journal entry written atomically on the user's shard → pub/sub wakes the user's notify box → other device's long-poll returns the journal entries after its cursor → it fetches the missing chunks from the CDN.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Save writes the change to a log, a push wakes your other device, and it pulls just the new pieces."
💬 *One-liner:* "Commit to journal, wake the device, pull the delta — usually under ten seconds."

### Failure
**❓ "What if the chunk-index (dedup KV) goes down?"**
*Mechanism:* the dedup check fails open — we treat all chunks as missing and upload them. Correctness is unaffected (content-addressing means duplicates collapse on write); we just over-upload temporarily.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Worst case we re-upload some bytes we already had — slower and pricier for a bit, never wrong."
💬 *One-liner:* "Dedup is an optimization, not correctness — losing it costs money, not data."

**❓ "A whole region's object store is unavailable — what does the user experience?"**
*Mechanism:* chunk reads fail over to a replicated region; new uploads buffer on the device and flush when the region heals. Metadata stays available from other-AZ replicas.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Downloads keep working from a backup region; your uploads wait safely on your device and sync once it's back."
💬 *One-liner:* "Bytes are multi-region, so a region outage is slower, not lossy."

**❓ "The notification service crashes — do users stop syncing?"**
*Mechanism:* no — sync degrades from push to a periodic long-poll; the change journal + cursor are the source of truth, so devices still catch up, just a few seconds staler.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Sync still works, it's just a touch slower while pushes are down."
💬 *One-liner:* "The journal is the truth; notifications only make sync faster, not possible."

### Scale / hot-spot
**❓ "Someone shares a folder with a million people and edits it — does that storm the DB?"**
*Mechanism:* above a member threshold the share flips from fan-out-on-write to fan-out-on-read; one edit is a single write to the shared resource's journal, and members merge it on their own sync cadence.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "For huge shares we don't copy the change to a million inboxes; people check the shared folder's own log when they sync."
💬 *One-liner:* "Hot shares flip to fan-out-on-read — one write regardless of audience size."

**❓ "What's your binding bottleneck at peak, and how do you relieve it?"**
*Mechanism:* metadata writes at ~580K/s vs ~15K/s per SQL node → shard by user_id across ~40 shards, making each commit single-shard; the new ceiling is per-shard write budget, relieved by adding shards.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "The database writes are the limit, so I split users across forty databases."
💬 *One-liner:* "Six hundred thousand metadata writes a second forces ~40 user-sharded nodes."

### Consistency
**❓ "Right after I upload, will my own device always see the file?"**
*Mechanism:* yes — read-your-own-writes: a device reading back its own just-committed file is routed to the shard primary, not a lagging replica.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Your own uploads always show up for you instantly; only *other* people's edits can lag a few seconds."
💬 *One-liner:* "Own-writes hit the primary; cross-user reads are eventual within the SLO."

**❓ "Can a retried commit create a duplicate file or double-count a chunk?"**
*Mechanism:* the commit carries an idempotency key; a retry returns the original version. Chunks are content-addressed so a re-PUT maps to the same key, and refcounts are bumped on commit, not on PUT, so retries don't double-count.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Retrying a save is safe — you get the same result, never a duplicate."
💬 *One-liner:* "Idempotency key plus content-addressing makes retries a no-op."

### Security
**❓ "How do you stop someone from accessing another user's files by guessing IDs?"**
*Mechanism:* the caller is always the JWT identity; every read/commit checks the Share/Permission store server-side against that identity. A guessed `file_id` on another user's shard fails authz.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "We check who you are from your login token on every request, not from anything the client claims."
💬 *One-liner:* "Identity comes from the JWT; we never trust a client-sent user or file id."

**❓ "Could someone forge a chunk to corrupt a shared file?"**
*Mechanism:* chunks are content-addressed — the key *is* the hash of the bytes — so a chunk whose content doesn't match its claimed hash is rejected on write. You can't substitute bytes under an existing hash.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "A chunk's name is a fingerprint of its contents, so you can't sneak different bytes under it."
💬 *One-liner:* "Content-addressing is tamper-evident by construction."

### Cost
**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system?"**
*Mechanism:* raw blob storage (~$150M/mo for ~6,500 PB) is the biggest absolute line, and egress (~$75M/mo) is the one the architecture actively fights with dedup, delta-sync, and CDN.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Storing the bytes costs the most overall; moving them is the cost we engineer hardest against."
💬 *One-liner:* "Storage is the biggest bill; bandwidth is the bill we design around."

**❓ "Where would you cut cost first?"**
*Mechanism:* cold-tier chunks untouched for >30 days to Glacier-class storage (most stored bytes are rarely re-read), and lean harder on dedup + delta-sync to shrink both storage and egress.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "Move rarely-touched files to cheap cold storage and make sure we never store or send a byte twice."
💬 *One-liner:* "Tier cold data and dedup harder — that hits both top line items."

### Extensibility
**❓ "How would you add full-text search inside files?"**
*Mechanism:* add an async indexing pipeline that consumes the change journal (it already has every mutation), extracts text, and writes to a search index (Elasticsearch-class) — no change to the sync hot path.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "The change log already lists everything that changed, so search just listens to it and builds an index on the side."
💬 *One-liner:* "Search is a journal consumer — bolt it on without touching sync."

**❓ "How would you add end-to-end encryption?"**
*Mechanism:* encrypt chunks client-side before hashing; cross-user dedup mostly disappears (different keys → different ciphertext → different hashes), but per-user dedup with a per-user key survives. We'd drop the global dedup assumption and re-cost storage.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "We'd encrypt before chunking, which means we can't dedup across users anymore — a privacy-vs-cost trade."
💬 *One-liner:* "E2E encryption kills cross-user dedup; that's the explicit trade-off."

**❓ "How would you support team/enterprise admin features like audit logs?"**
*Mechanism:* the per-user change journals already form an audit trail; an admin service aggregates journals across a team (via the team's membership in the Share store) and exposes a read-only view — again, a consumer of existing data.
🗣️ *In plain words:* "We already log every change per user, so admin audit is just a team-wide view of those logs."
💬 *One-liner:* "Audit logs fall out of the change journal for free."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "Dropbox is a service that keeps a user's files identical across all their devices and the cloud. The design splits into two halves. The **data half** is bytes: files are cut into content-defined chunks, named by their content hash so identical chunks are stored once, kept in an S3-class object store with eleven-nines durability and served through a CDN — that's how a tiny edit uploads kilobytes, not gigabytes. The **control half** is metadata: a SQL database sharded by user id holds each file's manifest, version history, and an append-only change journal, because a commit must atomically write the file and advance a per-user sequence, and at six hundred thousand writes a second that forces about forty shards. The hardest problem in the control half is concurrent offline edits, which I solve with version vectors and never-lose-data conflicted copies. The two halves connect through a change journal plus a long-poll notification fleet: a commit appends to the journal, a push wakes the user's other devices, and they pull just the changed chunks — under ten seconds end to end. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: never ship or store a byte twice, and never tell three hundred million devices to poll a database."
